skill.name: reviewing-dotnet-code
skill.description: Reviews C# and .NET code for Microsoft naming conventions, async patterns, exception handling, LINQ usage, and modern language features.
skill.summary: Review and improve C# and .NET code using Microsoft conventions and modern patterns.
skill.author: BrendanShields

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution and weak-crypto patterns, but manual review found Markdown backticks, headings, and C# examples rather than executable shell or Ruby code. No prompt injection, secret exfiltration, network callback, or malicious intent was found. The remaining risk is normal filesystem access for reviewing and editing code.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Are Markdown and C# Example False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick hits are Markdown inline code and fenced C# examples. The weak-crypto hits are words in headings or table-of-contents entries, not use of cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visibly Markdown tables, links, or C# code fences rather than executable Ruby, shell, or crypto code. No execution path exists in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access for Code Review
low_findings.description: The skill declares Read, Edit, Grep, and Glob tools so it can inspect and modify project files. This is expected for a code-review skill, but users should review proposed edits before applying them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool list explicitly grants file reading, searching, and editing capabilities. The access is aligned with the stated code review purpose and is not evidence of malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: C# reviews often miss style, async, and exception-handling issues. This skill applies Microsoft conventions and modern .NET patterns to reviews and refactors.
actual_capabilities: Checks C# naming conventions for classes, methods, fields, parameters, constants, and async methods.
Reviews async and await usage for blocking calls, async void, cancellation tokens, and library patterns.
Identifies exception-handling issues such as empty catches, broad catches, missing disposal, and lost stack traces.
Suggests modern C# features including file-scoped namespaces, records, target-typed new, and collection expressions.
Provides before-and-after refactoring guidance using examples from the included reference files.
Defers to project .editorconfig rules when local style preferences are available.
limitations: It focuses on C# and .NET style, structure, and common patterns, not full application security testing.
It does not compile, run tests, or verify runtime behavior unless the user asks and tools are available.
It may need project context before recommending architecture or library-level async changes.
It follows the included guidance and may not match every team-specific coding standard.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Find naming, async, exception-handling, and LINQ issues before merging C# changes.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy C#
use_cases.description: Modernize older .NET code with records, file-scoped namespaces, target-typed new, and clearer naming.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Guidance
use_cases.description: Apply consistent Microsoft-style conventions when generating services, DTOs, interfaces, and repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Basic C# Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one file or small change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this C# file for naming, async usage, exception handling, and modern .NET style. Prioritize clear findings with fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Suggestions
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve an existing class without changing behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Suggest a focused refactor for this .NET class. Preserve behavior and explain each naming, LINQ, async, or disposal improvement.

prompt_templates.title: Pull Request Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Review several changed C# files before merge.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these .NET changes like a pull request. Group findings by severity and include concise replacement guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Architecture-Aware Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review with project conventions and .editorconfig context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this .NET feature against the project conventions. Check .editorconfig first, then evaluate async boundaries, DTOs, repositories, and error handling.

output_examples.input: A service class blocks on an async repository call and catches all exceptions.
output: High: Replace blocking async access with an async method path to avoid deadlocks.
Medium: Catch specific exceptions and rethrow unexpected failures after logging.
Low: Rename fields and parameters to match C# conventions.

output_examples.input: A DTO class manually implements equality and mutable properties.
output: Suggestion: Convert the DTO to a record when immutable value equality is intended.
Benefit: The result is shorter, clearer, and follows modern C# data-transfer patterns.

output_examples.input: A reviewer asks whether a team should use String or string in new code.
output_examples.output: Use C# language keywords such as string, int, and bool unless a project convention says otherwise.

best_practices: Read project .editorconfig and existing conventions before suggesting style-only changes.
Separate correctness risks from readability improvements so reviewers can prioritize fixes.
Ask for surrounding code when async behavior, disposal, or exception flow depends on call context.
anti_patterns: Do not apply modern C# syntax when the project targets an older language version.
Do not recommend broad refactors when the user asked for a narrow review.
Do not treat style examples as proof that production code has the same issue.

faq.question: What languages does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on C# and .NET code, including services, DTOs, interfaces, repositories, LINQ, and async patterns.

faq.question: Can it edit code directly?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill declares file read, search, and edit tools for code review and refactoring workflows.

faq.question: Does it run tests?
faq.answer: Not by itself. It can recommend tests or run available commands when the user asks and the environment supports them.

faq.question: Does it follow Microsoft conventions?
faq.answer: Yes. The included guidance emphasizes Microsoft-style naming, type keywords, async patterns, exceptions, and modern C# usage.

faq.question: Will it respect team-specific style rules?
faq.answer: It instructs the assistant to check .editorconfig and defer to project rules when they exist.

faq.question: Is this a security review skill?
faq.answer: No. It can flag some risky coding patterns, but it is mainly a C# style and maintainability review skill.
